edges.description: The science of jurisprudence regards the state and power as something existing absolutely, while history views them as mere phenomena.
Power is defined as the collective will of the people transferred to their chosen rulers.
The theory of the transference of the collective will of the people to historic persons is unconfirmed by the experience of history.
Historians explain the relation of the people to their rulers in three ways: unconditional transfer, conditional transfer with known conditions, and conditional transfer with unknown conditions.
The first class of historians assumes the unconditional transference of the people's will to rulers, viewing opposing forces as mere violence.

The second class of historians believes that power rests on a conditional delegation of the will of the people to their rulers, but they often contradict each other regarding the nature of this program.
The third class of historians assumes that the will of the people is transferred conditionally, but the conditions are unknown.
The theory of power as the collective will of the people transferred to rulers fails to explain historical events such as revolutions and civil wars.
Experience shows that power is not merely a word but an actually existing phenomenon, evidenced by historical events.
The expression of a man's will is only part of the general activity expressed in an event, such as in a war or a revolution.
The connection between commands and events requires the continuity of movement in time and the inevitability of the connection between the person commanding and those executing the command.
The execution of commands is dependent on preceding orders that render the execution possible.

However, he is unaware that Moscow is largely deserted, with only a few inhabitants remaining. The narrative contrasts Napoleon's grandiose expectations with the reality of an empty city, likening it to a queenless hive, devoid of life and activity. The text also describes the chaotic departure of Russian troops and the looting by soldiers in the city, illustrating the disarray and abandonment of Moscow during this historical moment.
response.summary: The chunk explores Napoleon's thoughts as he arrives in a deserted Moscow, planning to rule with kindness, while unaware of the city's emptiness. It contrasts his expectations with the chaotic reality of looting soldiers and abandoned streets.

response.description: This chunk discusses the philosophical concepts of free will and inevitability, arguing that while natural sciences may explain human actions through physiological and zoological perspectives, they do not resolve the deeper questions of consciousness and freedom. It emphasizes that history provides a unique vantage point for understanding the interplay of free will and necessity, as historical events can be viewed as manifestations of both concepts. The text further explores how our perception of freedom and necessity fluctuates based on external influences, temporal context, and causal relationships, ultimately concluding that neither complete freedom nor complete necessity can be fully realized in human actions.
response.summary: The text explores the relationship between free will and inevitability, arguing that while natural sciences provide insights into human behavior, they do not fully address the complexities of consciousness. It posits that history uniquely captures the interplay of these concepts, revealing how our understanding of freedom and necessity varies based on context.

a2e5e2a5225a73aca88f6d2c333f690c75f04eca.user_input_preview: And that is how power is understood by the science of jurisprudence, that exchange bank of history which offers to exc

response.description: This chunk discusses the concept of power in jurisprudence and history, emphasizing the difference between how power is perceived in legal terms versus historical contexts. It critiques the theories of historians regarding the transfer of the collective will of the people to rulers, arguing that these theories fail to explain historical events accurately. The text examines the limitations of viewing historical figures as mere representatives of the people's will and highlights the complexities of power dynamics during revolutions and wars. It concludes that the relationship between commands and their execution is intricate and cannot be solely attributed to the will of leaders.
response.summary: The chunk explores the concept of power as understood in jurisprudence versus history, critiquing historians' theories on the transfer of the people's will to rulers. It argues that these theories inadequately explain historical events and emphasizes the complexities of power dynamics.
